## 2. Related Work

### 2.1 Regime Detection in Financial Markets

Regime-dependent modelling of financial time series traces to Hamilton's (1989) Markov switching framework and its volatility-regime extension by Hamilton and Susmel (1994); the volatility focus is directly relevant here, as §6.3 finds volatility-family features dominate the mutual-information ranking on EUR-USD 5m data. Modern HMM work emphasises *persistence* of inferred states: Nystrup et al. (2017) introduced time-varying transition probabilities for dynamic asset allocation, and Nystrup et al. (2020) penalised jumps in the HMM estimation to encourage persistent rather than transient state discovery. Palupi et al. (2021), Gopinathan et al. (2024), and Yang et al. (2025) extend the HMM line into international markets, GMM-HMM hybrids, and evolutionary alpha generation respectively. A shared limitation across this lineage is the decoupling: regime estimation optimises feature-space likelihood independently of the trading policy, with parameter adjustment applied post hoc.

Gaussian Mixture Models offer an alternative without imposed temporal transition structure (McLachlan & Peel, 2000); their soft probabilistic membership and per-regime covariance match the requirements of the downstream confidence-conditioned position sizer (§3.5). Hierarchical structure in market regimes has independent empirical support: Cont (2001) on nested distributional regularities and Orton & Gebbie (2024) on block-hierarchical regime detection motivate the two-level macro/sub decomposition in §3.2. Ang and Timmermann (2012) survey regime-switching models and explicitly distinguish regime *identification* (state-label estimation) from regime *application* (decisions conditioned on those labels); this research collapses that gap by making regime structure the *topology* of the evolutionary search itself.

### 2.2 Evolutionary Computation for Trading Systems

Early evolutionary trading work (Neely, Weller & Dittmar, 1997; Allen & Karjalainen, 1999) showed that evolved rules could appear profitable in-sample yet transfer poorly to live execution under realistic transaction costs — an early warning that the search problem is structurally harder than fitness alone suggests, and that evolutionary computation can fit training-data noise rather than the directional edge the GA's fitness function presumed. Aguilar-Rivera et al. (2015) survey GA, GP, MOEA, and EDA approaches across financial applications, identifying parameter sensitivity and overfitting as persistent challenges. Dempster & Jones (2001) addressed non-stationarity through online re-evolution (rolling re-training of one population) — a *temporal-recency* approach contrasting with this work's *regime-structure* approach (per-regime isolated populations on a fixed but regime-segmented training window).

Recent work pushes toward fuller-genome evolution and per-regime specialisation. Zhang et al. (2020) (AutoAlpha hierarchical alpha-factor search) and Long & Kampouridis (2025) (full multi-threshold parameter spaces for directional-changes strategies) confirm the trend toward complete-genome rather than restricted-subset evolution. Yang et al. (2025) is the closest prior work: a co-evolutionary GP framework with subpopulations evolving independently per market regime, sharing the per-regime evolutionary isolation principle but differing in representation (GP expressions vs fixed-parameter vectors), regime model (HMM-based vs hierarchical GMM), and application domain (alpha factor construction vs strategy execution parameters).

Reinforcement learning has become the dominant alternative paradigm: Liu et al. (2020) introduced FinRL as a deep RL framework for automated trading, with subsequent work (e.g. Bauman et al., 2024) extending it to regime-aware policies. The architectural distinction is structural: deep RL learns a single policy network mapping state-including-regime to action; this work's island-model GA maintains explicit, interpretable parameter populations per regime — trading representational capacity for interpretability and per-regime isolation.

Overfitting remains a paradigm-spanning concern (Bailey et al., 2014; McLean & Pontiff, 2016). Bailey et al. (2014) demonstrate that backtest-overfitting probability rises rapidly with trial count. We address it through per-regime evolution (each population sees a single market state, reducing the diversity each genome must accommodate) and strict hold-out validation (training through Dec 2024, evaluation from Jan 2025); CPCV adaptation to per-regime island populations (Lopez de Prado, 2018; Arian et al., 2024) is identified as future work.

### 2.3 Island-Model Genetic Algorithms

Island-model GAs maintain semi-isolated subpopulations with periodic migration, preserving diversity by letting different populations explore different regions of the search space (Whitley et al., 1999; Alba & Tomassini, 2002), grounded in Wright's (1931) shifting-balance theory. The migration topology — ring, star, random, or problem-specific — controls gene flow and the exploration-exploitation trade-off (Cantu-Paz, 2000). The relevant gap for this work is that topology selection in prior literature is largely empirical: islands are interchangeable subpopulations and the migration graph is chosen heuristically (Cantu-Paz, 2000; Lopes et al., 2012), or driven by parallelism rather than problem structure (Chideme, Chen & Lin, 2025).

Theoretically, the architecture connects to niching/fitness-sharing methods (Goldberg & Richardson, 1987; Mahfoud, 1995): subpopulation boundaries align with structural partitions of the fitness landscape, which is exactly the regime structure the hierarchical GMM discovers. To the best of our knowledge, this research is the first to use market regimes as the structuring principle for island topology in a trading strategy *execution-parameter* optimisation system evaluated under a realistic execution engine. Each island corresponds to a discovered market state, and sibling migration occurs between sub-regimes of the same macro-cluster. Yang et al. (2025) share the per-regime subpopulation concept but evolve GP expressions for alpha factors via co-evolutionary feedback, whereas this work evolves fixed-length parameter vectors via an island-model GA with ring-topology sibling migration, applied to execution parameters and evaluated on a production backtester with real transaction costs.

The contribution of this research is the *combination* of four components, each with separate prior art but not previously assembled: (a) hierarchical two-level GMM regime discovery with BIC-based component selection (§3.2), grounded in §2.1 and motivated by Orton & Gebbie (2024); (b) island migration topology *derived from* the regime hierarchy (§3.3), with sibling-only migration within macro-clusters and isolation across them — a problem-derived rather than heuristic partition; (c) per-regime evolution of the *complete* strategy parameter space (§3.4), extending the full-genome trend (Long & Kampouridis, 2025) with regime conditioning; and (d) fitness evaluation on the *production execution engine* with real per-candle OANDA bid-ask spread, swap, and slippage costs (§4), addressing the simulation-to-deployment transfer gap (Lopez de Prado, 2018). The empirical contribution (§6) is the demonstration that this combination collapses the maximum drawdown of a grid-hedged Martingale strategy on EUR-USD by approximately two orders of magnitude under realistic execution costs.

### 2.4 Grid-Hedged Martingale Strategies and Risk Management

Grid-hedged Martingale strategies open additional positions at fixed price intervals against an adverse move, with take-profit targets designed to recover the aggregate position's cost basis (Doob, 1953; Carricaburu, c. 2004); Rundo et al. (2019) introduced the GTSBot grid trading system, one of the few peer-reviewed academic treatments of grid execution on FX and a baseline for the present work (§5). The fundamental property is that these strategies trade a high probability of small wins for a small probability of catastrophic loss. For a grid-hedged variant with maximum depth *N*, geometric multiplier *m*, and per-level loss probability *p*, expected loss upon bust grows as a geometric series in *m* while the probability of reaching successive depths compounds approximately as *p^k*. The critical quantity is the product *p* × *m*: when *p* × *m* < 1.0 the strategy is asymptotically convergent under repeated cycles; when *p* × *m* > 1.0, expected loss diverges with depth and ruin is structurally guaranteed in the limit (Doob, 1953; Ethier, 2010). The bound applies directly to grid-hedged execution on liquid spot instruments where each entry incurs spread cost.

Martingale doubling is suboptimal compared to Kelly-style proportional sizing (Kelly, 1956; Thorp, 1969; MacLean, Thorp & Ziemba, 2010): proportional sizing is growth-rate optimal under known probabilities, while doubling exposes the bettor to ruin in finite time even on positive-expectancy games. Yet Martingale-family strategies remain widely deployed in retail and algorithmic forex because per-cycle win rates are high and failures are rare but catastrophic. The modern financial-machine-learning literature frames position sizing, backtest design, and the simulation-to-deployment gap as integrated methodology concerns (Lopez de Prado, 2018), directly relevant to this work's real-engine fitness evaluation choice (§4). This combination — high cycle win rates, rare but catastrophic failures, extreme sensitivity to misconfiguration — makes Martingale-family strategies a stringent test case for adaptive risk management.
